source.title: Enhancing the biocatalytic manufacture of the key intermediate of atorvastatin by focused directed evolution of halohydrin dehalogenase

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: The engineered halohydrin dehalogenase HheC2360, previously developed for manufacturing ethyl (R)-4-cyano-3-hydroxybutanoate (HN), showed insufficient activity for cyanolysis of tert-butyl (3R,5S)-6-cyano-3,5-dihydroxyhexanoate (A7) in an alternative atorvastatin intermediate route. The goal was to enhance HheC2360 activity toward the larger substrate D3 via focused directed evolution targeting active site residues identified by docking.

section_gists.heading: Enhancing the biocatalytic manufacture of the key intermediate of atorvastatin by focused directed evolution of halohydrin dehalogenase
section_gists.gist: Introduction and background: HHDHs are valuable for C-C, C-N, C-O, C-S bond formation. HheC from Agrobacterium radiobacter AD1 was engineered to HheC2360 (18 rounds of directed evolution) for HN production. Atorvastatin synthesis routes (KRED and DERA) are described. The DERA route was extended, but HheC2360 had insufficient activity for cyanolysis of D3 to A7. Focused directed evolution of HheC2360 active site was performed to improve activity.
process_signals: HheC2360 previously engineered for HN manufacture had insufficient activity for D3 substrate.
Focused directed evolution targeting active site residues was chosen as strategy.
Docking of product A7 into HheC2360 structure guided residue selection.
methods: Docking of A7 into HheC2360 crystal structure (PDB: 4IXT)
failures_or_limits: HheC2360 insufficient activity for D3 substrate

section_gists.heading: Results
section_gists.gist: Docking identified 6 residues (V84, W86, W139, L142, Y186, Y187) within 5 Å of A7, grouped for CASTing. Library screening (3072 colonies) of V84-W86 library yielded 14 beneficial mutants; all contained W86 mutation. Single mutants W86V/L/I showed ~5-fold activity increase. Best double mutants: V84G/W86V (9-fold) and V84G/W86F (15-fold). Kinetic analysis: V84G/W86F had Km 1.7 mM, kcat 671 s-1, kcat/Km 390 s-1 mM-1 (16-fold over HheC2360). Time-course bioconversion: V84G/W86F showed 3-fold faster A7 formation, 1.8-fold faster D3 consumption, full conversion in 24 h vs 44 h for HheC2360.
process_signals: Docking revealed steric hindrance between V84/W86 and t-butyl group of A7.
CASTing libraries constructed for three residue groups.
Screening of V84-W86 library identified beneficial mutants; all had W86 mutation.
Single W86 mutants gave ~5-fold improvement; double mutants V84G/W86V and V84G/W86F gave 9- and 15-fold.
Kinetic constants confirmed increased kcat and decreased Km.
Bioconversion time course showed 2-fold space-time productivity improvement.
methods: Docking A7 into HheC2360 (PDB: 4IXT) with RMSD minimization of hydroxypropionitrile motif
CASTing: grouped residues V84-W86, W139-L142, Y186-Y187
High-throughput screening via chloride ion colorimetric assay (iron(III)/thiocyanate, A460)
Time-course bioconversion with cell lysates (20 mg/mL), HPLC monitoring
metrics: Substrate consumption, product formation, epoxide intermediate over time
failures_or_limits: Most combined mutants showed similar or lower activity than single mutants, except V84G/W86V and V84G/W86F.
Bioconversion rates of cell lysates not fully consistent with initial activity differences.

section_gists.heading: Discussion
section_gists.gist: Engineered biocatalysts for one product may not meet requirements for another due to substrate structural variations. HheC2360 had low activity for D3 because of steric hindrance from V84 and W86 with the t-butyl group. Docking identified 14 residues near A7; after excluding catalytic, halide-binding, and oligomer-stabilizing residues, 6 were chosen for CASTing. V84 and W86 were closest (3.3–3.5 Å) and mutations to smaller hydrophobic residues relieved steric clash. Single W86 mutations improved activity; double mutants with V84G further enhanced it. Hydrophilic mutations were unfavorable. The V84G/W86F variant showed 16-fold catalytic efficiency improvement and 2-fold bioconversion rate increase, demonstrating applicability for atorvastatin intermediate production.
process_signals: Rationale for residue selection: excluded catalytic triad (S132, Y145, R149), halide-binding aromatics (F12, Y186, Y187), T134A (already present), N176 (H-bond to new hydroxyl), W192 (oligomer stability), W201 (already mutated).
Steric hindrance between V84/W86 and t-butyl group was key limitation.
Mutation to smaller hydrophobic residues (V, I, L, F, etc.) preferred; hydrophilic mutations (S, T) less effective.
V84G/W86F achieved 16-fold kcat/Km increase and 2-fold faster bioconversion.
failures_or_limits: Mutations of Y186-Y187 and W139-L142 not reported; likely no significant improvement or not pursued.
V84S/W86V and V84T/W86I were less effective than hydrophobic counterparts.

section_gists.heading: Materials and Methods
section_gists.gist: Experimental details: codon-optimized hheC2360 gene cloned into pWF-1 for library construction and pET28a for expression. Mutant libraries constructed by QuikChange PCR with degenerate primers. Screening in 96-well plates using chloride colorimetric assay. Kinetic parameters determined by varying D3 concentration. Protein expression in E. coli BL21(DE3) at 16°C, purification via Ni-IDA. Substrate D3 prepared from D2 (DERA-catalyzed) via esterification. Bioconversion: 8 g D3 in 50 mL cell lysate (20 mg/mL), 1.2 eq NaCN, pH 7.0, 45°C, 24 h, monitored by HPLC.
process_signals: Library screening used OD600 to normalize protein concentration due to similar expression levels.
Colorimetric assay based on chloride release enabled high-throughput screening.
Substrate D3 synthesis involved DERA and chemical esterification.

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: HheC2360, optimized for HN, has a binding pocket too small for the bulkier D3 substrate, causing steric hindrance and low activity.
Focused directed evolution targeting residues identified by docking can efficiently enhance activity for the new substrate.
Mutations that reduce steric clash (smaller hydrophobic residues at V84 and W86) will improve activity.
Combinatorial mutations (V84G/W86F) can synergistically improve both Km and kcat.
uncertainties_to_resolve: Were the W139-L142 and Y186-Y187 libraries screened? If so, what were the outcomes?
Why were only V84-W86 library results reported in detail?
What is the enantioselectivity of the variant for A7?
Is the variant stable under process conditions (45°C, 24 h)?
How does the variant perform at higher substrate loadings?
What is the scalability of the bioconversion process?

events.summary: HheC2360 had insufficient activity for cyanolysis of D3 to A7.
Focused directed evolution of HheC2360 active site was performed to enhance activity.
Docking of A7 into HheC2360 crystal structure identified 6 residues within 5 Å.
CASTing libraries were constructed and screened; 14 beneficial mutants identified from V84-W86 library.
Bioconversion time course showed V84G/W86F achieved full conversion in 24 h, 2-fold faster than HheC2360.
Bioconversion rates of cell lysates not fully consistent with initial activity differences.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers introduction, problem statement, design, docking, library screening, kinetic analysis, and bioconversion validation.

events.summary: Residues V84, W86, L142, W139, Y186, Y187 were chosen for CASTing after excluding catalytic, halide-binding, and oligomer-stabilizing residues.
Docking revealed strong steric hindrance between V84/W86 and the t-butyl group of A7, with distances of 3.3 Å (W86) and 3.5 Å (V84).
Single mutations of W86 (W86V, W86L, W86I) gave 5.0-fold activity improvement.
Double mutant V84G/W86F exhibited highest activity (15-fold) among tested variants.
Kinetic analysis showed decreasing Km and increasing kcat for variants, indicating improved affinity and enlarged binding space.
Screening of W139/L142 and Y186/Y187 libraries failed to obtain mutants with increased activity.
V84G/W86F showed 2-fold increase in productivity in cyanolysis reaction, making the process more economically feasible.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains detailed design rationale, experimental results, and validation of the engineered variant.

events.summary: Enzymatic cyanolysis of D3 to A7 was performed using cell lysates of HheC2360 or its mutants.
HPLC analysis monitored substrate D3, epoxide intermediate, and product A7 during bioconversion.
Chemical conversion of A7 to A8 was achieved using 2,2-dimethoxypropane and p-toluenesulfonic acid.
Docking of product A7 into HheC structure (PDB: 4IXT) was performed to identify interacting residues.

chunk_events.notes: This chunk primarily describes experimental methods for substrate preparation, bioconversion, product isolation, and chemical conversion, plus a docking protocol. It does not contain new process events like problem identification or hypothesis generation, but provides experimental details supporting earlier events.

event_graph.paper_main_problem: HheC2360 had insufficient activity for cyanolysis of D3 to A7.

canonical_events.summary: HheC2360 had insufficient activity for cyanolysis of D3 to A7.
Focused directed evolution of HheC2360 active site was performed to enhance activity.
Docking of A7 into HheC2360 crystal structure identified 6 residues within 5 Å.
Residues V84, W86, L142, W139, Y186, Y187 were chosen for CASTing after excluding catalytic, halide-binding, and oligomer-stabilizing residues.
Docking revealed strong steric hindrance between V84/W86 and the t-butyl group of A7, with distances of 3.3 Å (W86) and 3.5 Å (V84).
CASTing libraries were constructed and screened; 14 beneficial mutants identified from V84-W86 library.
Single mutations of W86 (W86V, W86L, W86I) gave 5.0-fold activity improvement.
Double mutant V84G/W86F exhibited highest activity (15-fold) among tested variants.
Kinetic analysis showed decreasing Km and increasing kcat for variants, indicating improved affinity and enlarged binding space.
Screening of W139/L142 and Y186/Y187 libraries failed to obtain mutants with increased activity.
Bioconversion time course showed V84G/W86F achieved full conversion in 24 h, 2-fold faster than HheC2360.
Bioconversion rates of cell lysates not fully consistent with initial activity differences.

edges.reason: Insufficient activity of HheC2360 motivates focused directed evolution.
Focused directed evolution requires identification of target residues via docking.
Docking identifies candidate residues, which are then filtered for CASTing.
Selection of residues leads to detailed analysis of steric hindrance.
Understanding steric hindrance guides library construction and screening.
Screening identifies beneficial single mutants, which are characterized.
Single mutants are combined to yield double mutant with highest activity.
Best variant is kinetically characterized to understand improvement.
While V84-W86 library yielded hits, other libraries failed.
Best variant is validated in bioconversion time course.
Bioconversion results reveal inconsistency with initial activity, a limitation.

must_cover_points: Docking to identify active site residues
